analogy: Think of "Theory of the Firm" through the chapter's own opening images: Chapter 4 The Theor The Theor The Theor The Theor The Theory of the F y of the F y of the F y of the F y of the Firm irm irm irm irm under P under P under P under P under Per... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 4.1 PERFECT COMPETITION: DEFINING FEATURES, 4.2 REVENUE, 55 The Theory of the Firm, AR = TR. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Theory of the Firm is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that chapter 4 The Theor The Theor The Theor The Theor The Theory of the F y of the F y of the F y of the F y of the Firm irm irm irm irm under P under P under P under P under Per er er er erfect Competition fect Competition fect Competition fect Competition fect Competition In the previous chapter, we studied concepts related to a firm’s production function and cost curves.

The text explains that here we ask : how does a firm decide how much to produce?

The text explains that our answer to this question is by no means simple or uncontroversial.

The text explains that so, the amount that a firm produces and sells in the market is that which maximises its profit.

The text explains that here, we also assume that the firm sells whatever it produces so that ‘output’ and quantity sold are often used interchangebly.

The text explains that we first set up and examine in detail the profit maximisation problem of a firm.

The text explains that the supply curve shows the levels of output that a firm chooses to produce at different market prices.

The text explains that finally, we study how to aggregate the supply curves of individual firms and obtain the market supply curve.

The text explains that 4.1 PERFECT COMPETITION: DEFINING FEATURES In order to analyse a firm’s profit maximisation problem, we must first specify the market environment in which the firm functions.

The text explains that in this chapter, we study a market environment called perfect competition.

The text explains that a perfectly competitive market has the following defining features: 1.

The text explains that the market consists of a large number of buyers and sellers 2.

The text explains that each firm produces and sells a homogenous product. i.e., the product of one firm cannot be differentiated from the product of any other firm.

The text explains that entry into the market as well as exit from the market are free for firms.

The text explains that the existence of a large number of buyers and sellers means that each individual buyer and seller is very small compared to the size of the market.

The text explains that this means that no individual buyer or seller can influence the market by their size.

The text explains that these features result in the single most distinguishing characteristic of perfect competition: price taking behaviour.

The text explains that price-taking is often thought to be a reasonable assumption when the market has many firms and buyers have perfect information about the price prevailing in the market.

The text explains that observe that since all firms produce the same good and all buyers are aware of the market price, the firm in question loses all its buyers.

The text explains that but, if this is the case, surely there is no reason to set a price lower than the market price.

The text explains that the critical question is: how do we identify q0?

The text explains that to answer this question, refer once again to Figure 4.3.

The text explains that it is expected that this will lower the firm’s marginal cost at any level of output; that is, there is a rightward (or downward) shift of the MC curve.

The text explains that as the firm’s supply curve is essentially a segment of the MC curve, technological progress shifts the supply curve of the firm to the right.

The text explains that impact of a unit tax on supply A unit tax is a tax that the government imposes per unit sale of output.

The text explains that for example, suppose that the unit tax imposed by the government is Rs 2.

The text explains that then, if the firm produces and sells 10 units of the good, the total tax that the firm must pay to the government is 10 × Rs 2 = Rs 20.

The text explains that now, suppose the government puts in place a unit tax of Rs t.

The text explains that the two firms have different cost structures.

The text explains that specifically, if the number of firms in the market increases (decreases), the market supply curve shifts to the right (left).

The text explains that 67 The Theory of the Firm under Perfect Competition • In a perfectly competitive market, firms are price-takers. • The total revenue of a firm is the market price of the good multiplied by the firm’s output of the good. • For a price-taking firm, average revenue is equal to market price. • For a price-taking firm, marginal revenue is equal to market price. • The demand curve that a firm faces in a perfectly competitive market is perfectly elastic; it is a horizontal straight line at the market price. • The profit of a firm is the difference between total revenue earned and total cost incurred. • If there is a positive level of output at which a firm’s profit is maximised in the short run, three conditions must hold at that output level (i) p = SMC (ii) SMC is non-decreasing (iii) p ≥ AV C. • If there is a positive level of output at which a firm’s profit is maximised in the long run, three conditions must hold at that output level (i) p = LRMC (ii) LRMC is non-decreasing (iii) p ≥ LRAC. • The short run supply curve of a firm is the rising part of the SMC curve from and above minimum AVC together with 0 output for all prices less than the minimum AVC. • The long run supply curve of a firm is the rising part of the LRMC curve from and above minimum LRAC together with 0 output for all prices less than the minimum LRAC. • Technological progress is expected to shift the supply curve of a firm to the right. • An increase (decrease) in input prices is expected to shift the supply curve of a firm to the left (right). • The imposition of a unit tax shifts the supply curve of a firm to the left. • The market supply curve is obtained by the horizontal summation of the supply curves of individual firms. • The price elasticity of supply of a good is the percentage change in quantity supplied due to one per cent change in the market price of the good.

The text explains that a firm earns a revenue of Rs 50 when the market price of a good is Rs 10.

The text explains that the market price increases to Rs 15 and the firm now earns a revenue of Rs 150.

The text explains that what is the price elasticity of the firm’s supply curve?

The text explains that the market price of a good changes from Rs 5 to Rs 20.

The text explains that as a result, the quantity supplied by a firm increases by 15 units.

The text explains that the price elasticity of the firm’s supply curve is 0.5.

The text explains that find the initial and final output levels of the firm.

The text explains that at the market price of Rs 10, a firm supplies 4 units of output.

The text explains that the price elasticity of the firm’s supply is 1.25.

The text explains that what quantity will the firm supply at the new price? ? ?

Source: NCERT Class 12 Introductory Microeconomics, Chapter 4: Theory of the Firm

So, the amount that a firm produces and sells in the market is that which maximises its profit.
In other words π = TR – TC Clearly, the gap between TR and TC is the firm’s earnings net of costs.

prelims_traps: Do not treat 4.1 PERFECT COMPETITION: DEFINING FEATURES and 4.2 REVENUE as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Theory of the Firm; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Theory of the Firm through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 4.1 PERFECT COMPETITION: DEFINING FEATURES and 4.2 REVENUE to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
